core_claim: Fragnesia (CVE-2026-46300) is the third Linux kernel LPE in two weeks using page-cache corruption. It targets XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem. Same mitigation as Dirty Frag (blocklist esp4, esp6, rxrpc). No host-level privileges required unlike Dirty Frag. PoC released by V12 Security.

caption:
Fragnesia (CVE-2026-46300) is the third Linux kernel LPE in two weeks using page-cache corruption. Targets XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem. Discovered by William Bowling of V12 Security.

Separate bug from Dirty Frag but same surface and same mitigation. Deterministic page-cache corruption. No race condition. Immediate root on all major distros by corrupting /usr/bin/su page cache.

Unlike Dirty Frag, no host-level privileges required. AppArmor namespace restrictions are only partial mitigation, and additional bypasses are needed.

The pattern: Copy Fail (algif_aead) -> Dirty Frag (xfrm-ESP + RxRPC) -> Fragnesia (xfrm ESP-in-TCP). Three distinct subsystems. Same page-cache corruption primitive. Same root via /usr/bin/su. Same mitigation: blocklist esp4, esp6, rxrpc.

CloudLinux: Customers with Dirty Frag mitigation need no further action until patched kernels. Red Hat assessing if mitigation extends. Microsoft urges same mitigations. No in-the-wild exploitation observed yet.

Threat actor 'berz0k' advertising Linux LPE zero-day for $170,000 on cybercrime forums. Claims TOCTOU-based, stable, no crashes, .so payload in /tmp.

The page-cache corruption bug class spans three subsystems now. One mitigation covers all three. But the kernel keeps finding new paths to the same primitive.

How many page-cache corruption primitives are in your kernel right now?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[Fragnesia, CVE-2026-46300, Linux kernel, local privilege escalation, page cache, XFRM ESP-in-TCP, Dirty Frag, Copy Fail, kernel hardening, V12 Security]
comment_prompt: How many page-cache corruption primitives are in your kernel right now?
alt_text: Title card: Third page-cache root exploit in two weeks. Fragnesia is CVE-2026-46300 targeting XFRM ESP-in-TCP, no host privileges required, Dirty Frag mitigation covers it. Three shards fuse into one red blade.

Fragnesia is a separate bug from Dirty Frag but shares the same attack surface. CVE-2026-46300, CVSS 7.8, discovered by William Bowling of V12 Security, deterministic corruption with no race condition. A cluster of three red nodes glows against a dark field.

The exploit grants root without any host-level privileges by corrupting the /usr/bin/su page cache. AppArmor namespace restrictions are only partial mitigation. A scalpel makes a precise cut into a sealed locked block.

A logic bug in the ESP-in-TCP subsystem allows arbitrary writes into read-only page cache, no race condition required, root via /usr/bin/su corruption. A TCP packet unseals in place over glowing shared page-cache pages.

Three different subsystems, one identical page-cache corruption primitive. Copy Fail hit algif-aead, Dirty Frag hit xfrm-ESP and RxRPC, Fragnesia hits xfrm ESP-in-TCP. An assembly line produces the identical root exploit from three distinct inputs.

One blocklist covers all three exploits: esp4, esp6, rxrpc. Restrict user namespaces, harden containers, monitor for privilege escalation, patch when available. A single barrier of light seals three converging red breach paths.

Page-cache corruption now spans three subsystems. One mitigation covers all three, but the kernel keeps finding new paths to the same primitive. Three roots of light converge into a single dark trunk with fresh roots quietly sprouting below.
Closing question: How many page-cache corruption primitives are in your kernel right now? Three thin red threads converge into a single bright node on a clean dark end-card.

narration.text: Fragnesia is the third Linux kernel local privilege escalation in two weeks using the same page-cache corruption technique.
Discovered by William Bowling of V12 Security, it targets the XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem and is a separate bug from Dirty Frag, though it shares the same attack surface.
The exploit writes arbitrary bytes into the kernel page cache of read-only files, corrupting /usr/bin/su to get root with no host-level privileges required.
The pattern is now clear: Copy Fail hit the algif-aead subsystem, Dirty Frag hit xfrm-ESP and RxRPC, and Fragnesia hits xfrm ESP-in-TCP, three distinct paths to the exact same primitive.
Blocklisting esp4, esp6, and rxrpc kernel modules shuts down all three exploits, and CloudLinux confirms that customers who applied the Dirty Frag mitigation are already covered.
Separately, a threat actor going by berz0k is advertising an unrelated Linux local privilege escalation zero-day for $170,000 on cybercrime forums, claiming it is TOCTOU-based and stable.
The broader lesson is that the page-cache corruption bug class now spans three subsystems, and one mitigation covers all of them, but the kernel keeps surfacing new paths to the same outcome.
How many page-cache corruption primitives are sitting in your kernel right now? Drop your answer in the comments.

beats.caption: Third page-cache LPE in two weeks. Same root.
V12 Security discovery. Separate bug. Same surface.
No host privileges needed. AppArmor only partial.
Three subsystems. One primitive. algif_aead, xfrm UDP, xfrm TCP.
Same mitigation covers all three. berz0k selling $170k zero-day.
Blocklist esp4/esp6/rxrpc. Harden containers. Patch kernel.
Bug class spans three subsystems. Kernel finds new paths.
How many primitives in your kernel?
